title: Test Your Understanding: Load Balancing
description: Check your grasp of load balancing algorithms, L4 vs L7, and health checks!

questions.question: Which problem does a load balancer NOT directly solve?

options.text: Routing traffic away from unhealthy servers
Giving clients one stable address for many servers
Making a single-threaded app use multiple CPU cores

questions.explanation: A load balancer distributes traffic, provides availability via health checks, and abstracts the fleet behind one address. It does not change how an individual process uses CPU.

questions.question: Which algorithm is best when requests vary widely in how long they take to process?
questions.explanation: Least connections routes to the server with the fewest active connections, so a server stuck on slow requests stops receiving new ones. Round robin would keep piling work on a busy server.

questions.question: What is the key difference between L4 and L7 load balancing?

options.text: L4 is for databases, L7 is for web servers
L4 routes connections by IP/port without reading content; L7 understands HTTP and can route on path, headers, and cookies
L7 is always faster than L4
L4 supports HTTPS and L7 does not

questions.explanation: L4 works at the transport layer (TCP/UDP), forwarding connections without inspecting them. L7 parses the application protocol (HTTP) and can make content-aware routing decisions, at a small cost in overhead.

questions.question: An L7 load balancer can route requests to different backend pools based on the URL path (e.g. /api vs /images).
questions.explanation: Because an L7 balancer understands HTTP, it can read the path and route accordingly. An L4 balancer cannot, since it never inspects the request content.

questions.question: What is the main job of a health check?

options.text: To encrypt traffic between client and server
To let the load balancer detect unhealthy servers and stop routing to them

questions.explanation: Health checks let the balancer know which servers are alive and able to serve, so it can eject failing ones and restore them when they recover.

questions.question: Why is a health check that always returns 200 (regardless of real state) a problem?

options.text: The balancer keeps sending traffic to a server that may be unable to actually serve requests
It prevents round robin from working

questions.explanation: If the check lies (returns 200 even when the database is down), the balancer can't tell the server is broken and keeps routing real traffic to it, causing failures. A good check reflects whether the server can do its core job.

questions.question: In production, the load balancer itself should be made redundant.
questions.explanation: If all traffic flows through a single load balancer, it becomes a single point of failure. Production setups use redundant balancers (or a managed, replicated service) with failover.

questions.question: Which type of request is risky to automatically retry on another server after a failure?

options.text: An idempotent GET that reads data
A non-idempotent POST that may have partially succeeded

questions.explanation: Retrying a non-idempotent operation (like a payment POST) can cause it to run twice. Idempotent reads are safe to retry; mutations need care or idempotency keys.
